title: Browser Trust, CORS, CSRF, Cookies, and Security Headers · Contract Lens: Cors Not Authorization
summary: A browser API reflects origins, combines credentials with wildcard sharing, treats CORS as access control, relies only on SameSite, and emits weak cookie and framing policy. Planned artifact: Add and verify the httpsts cors not authorization increment in browser request and response-sharing policy matrix. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Separate browser response-sharing controls from server authentication, authorization, network clients, and cross-origin request sending.
Implement an exact origin allowlist, preflight method and field checks, credentials, exposed fields, Vary, cache duration, null origin policy, and failure behavior.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Node and Express server Predict Cors Not Authorization · Theory Cors Not Authorization
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory team handles case 6164 in a multi-tenant case service with a bounded PostgreSQL pool. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to keep CORS response sharing distinct from CSRF mutation authorization; preserve response ownership after a client abort; then lower a byte or time budget and prove rejection occurs at its owner. Browser code models pure decisions only. Node, Express, listener, TLS, PostgreSQL, proxy, worker, load, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Separate browser response-sharing controls from server authentication, authorization, network clients, and cross-origin request sending.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Cors Not Authorization contract for the supplied data.
